did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) as the classification model and Delaunay triangulation for feature extraction to classify skin cancer types (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, and Nevus) using the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) combined with Delaunay triangulation for skin cancer classification. It includes specific sections on Methodology (dataset description, data preprocessing, modeling), Results (accuracy and loss metrics for training, validation, and testing sets), and a Conclusion comparing the proposed model against a CNN baseline, which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Classification using Delaunay Triangulation and Graph Convolutional Network
journal: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
brief_summary: This study proposes a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) combined with Delaunay triangulation to classify five types of skin cancer: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, and Nevus. The researchers utilized the ISIC dataset obtained via Kaggle, employing Delaunay triangulation for boundary extraction to isolate cancerous lesions from surrounding skin. The model was trained on the training set and evaluated using validation and testing sets to assess performance and overfitting. However, the results indicated that the proposed model tended to overfit and failed to generate correct predictions for new images. The authors attributed this poor performance to class imbalance, insufficient features, and noise in the data, concluding that the model was unable to accurately diagnose skin cancer in this implementation.

all_task_types.target: Five types of skin cancer: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, and Nevus.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the use of a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) as a classification model to predict discrete diagnostic categories (skin cancer types) from the ISIC dataset. The task is described as 'multilabel image classification' in the keywords and involves predicting one of five specific classes. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and loss metrics for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset (via Kaggle)
datasets_used.description: A dataset collected by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) through the Kaggle platform, containing skin cancer images from various sources including Hospital Clínic de Barcelona, Medical University of Vienna, Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Melanoma Institute Australia, The University of Queensland, and the University of Athens Medical School.

use.training: Used for modeling process after data augmentation to balance classes.
use.validation: Used for model evaluation to check for overfitting/underfitting and model modification.
use.testing: Used to predict outcomes and evaluate final model performance.
use.other: The original dataset contained 2239 training images and 118 testing images across nine classes; five classes were excluded due to insufficient RAM/memory resources, leaving 1380 images for the study.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and loss metrics, and comparisons between the GCN and CNN models were made based on these descriptive statistics without statistical significance testing.

GCN Model.notes: The model tended to overfit and was unable to generate correct predictions for new skin cancer images. The accuracy of the validation process increased or decreased significantly during the modeling process.

CNN Model.notes: The CNN model outperformed the proposed GCN model by 30% in accuracy. However, the CNN model also overfitted, as the accuracy and loss of the validation process stopped improving after a certain amount of iteration while training metrics continued to improve.

ISIC Dataset (5-class subset).Comparison Notes: The accuracy of the CNN model both in the training and validation process outperformed the proposed GCN model by 30%. The gap between the training and validation processes' loss values is similar for both CNN and GCN models. However, the loss value of the proposed model is higher.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Delaunay triangulation used to extract boundary points and generate graph representation (nodes and adjacency matrices) from skin cancer images.
architecture_details.input_representation: Pixels and adjacency matrix of all images in the training set.
architecture_details.model_type: Graph-based neural network leveraging node messaging and interaction between regions/structures in an image.

training_details.data_augmentation: Performed on the training set to balance classes.

preprocessing_techniques: Data splitting into training, validation, and testing sets
Data augmentation to balance the training set classes
Conversion of images to Grayscale color space
Point extraction (100 points) from image contours
Convex hull computation using Quickhull Algorithm
Feature extraction using Delaunay Triangulation to generate graph representations (nodes and adjacency matrices)
Extraction of average pixel values for each Delaunay triangle

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to duplicate data and increase variety for balancing minority classes.

The transformation process was carried out using the ImageDataGenerator() function from the Tensorflow Keras library to balance the training set by increasing the size of minority classes to match the largest class.

conclusions: The proposed Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) model combined with Delaunay triangulation failed to accurately diagnose skin cancer, tending to overfit and unable to generate correct predictions for new images.

The GCN model's performance was significantly inferior to the baseline Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model, with the CNN outperforming the GCN by approximately 30% in accuracy (CNN validation accuracy of 60.14% vs. GCN validation accuracy of 32%).

The poor performance of the GCN model was attributed to several factors: class imbalance in the dataset (e.g., Actinic Keratosis and Dermatofibroma represented only 8% and 7% of the data, respectively), insufficient features for reliable prediction, inappropriate feature mapping by Delaunay triangulation for some images, and noise in the data.

The CNN model also exhibited overfitting, as its training accuracy continued to improve while validation accuracy and loss stabilized or worsened, indicating a failure to generalize properly to unseen data.

While GCN theoretically offers advantages in modeling interactions between regions and structures in medical images compared to CNNs, this specific implementation did not yield better results than the traditional CNN approach for this task.
important_remarks: The proposed GCN model failed to accurately diagnose skin cancer, exhibiting severe overfitting and inability to generalize to new images (validation accuracy of 32%).
The GCN model significantly underperformed the baseline CNN model, with the CNN achieving 60.14% validation accuracy compared to the GCN's 32%.

The study excluded four classes (Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion) from the full ISIC dataset due to insufficient RAM/memory resources, limiting the scope of the classification task.
Class imbalance was a significant limitation, with minority classes Actinic Keratosis (8%) and Dermatofibroma (7%) being underrepresented in the 1,380-image subset used.

The Delaunay triangulation feature extraction method demonstrated a specific weakness for the Nevus class, generating triangles in the background rather than the area of interest, which likely contributed to poor performance for that class.

The baseline CNN model also exhibited overfitting, as indicated by the divergence between training and validation loss/accuracy metrics, suggesting that the dataset or task complexity posed generalization challenges for both architectures.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance comparisons were based solely on descriptive accuracy and loss metrics.

The dataset was obtained via Kaggle from the ISIC archive, comprising images from multiple international sources (e.g., Hospital Clínic de Barcelona, Melanoma Institute Australia), but the specific demographic distribution of the 2000+ patients is not detailed.
